title: Use a Knife Safely
summary: Hold a knife correctly, protect your fingers, and make clean cuts while preparing food.
learnerPromise: Cut food safely and quickly without risking your fingers.
whyItMatters: A sharp knife used well is safer than a dull one used poorly. Good knife skills speed up prep time and make cooking more enjoyable.
realLifeUses: Chop vegetables for soups and salads
Slice bread, meat, or fruit neatly
Prepare ingredients evenly so they cook at the same rate
youWillLearn: How to hold a chef's knife by the handle, not the blade
The claw grip to protect your fingertips
How to slice with a smooth rocking motion
How to keep a knife sharp and when to hone it
miniChallenge: Practice the claw grip while slicing a carrot or cucumber.
steps: Place a damp towel under your cutting board so it does not slide.
Hold the knife handle firmly with your dominant hand, resting your index finger on the side of the blade near the top.
Curl the fingertips of your other hand inward, tucking them under your knuckles — this is the claw grip.
Place the flat side of the vegetable against the board and slice downward while moving your fingers back slightly after each cut.
Continue slicing slowly and steadily, keeping the knife tip on the board and rocking the blade up and down.
completionCriteria: Slices are mostly even in thickness
Fingers stay curled and protected throughout
commonProblems: Cutting board slides — place a damp towel underneath for grip
Pieces come out uneven — slow down and focus on consistent thickness
Knife feels dull — a dull knife slips more easily; hone it or ask someone to help sharpen it
tips: Start slowly; speed comes with practice
Use a sharp knife — it takes less force and gives you more control
Cut round vegetables in half first to create a flat, stable side
